issues.description: VAL-RING-004 (ring latency < 122 us/call) is NOT met — ring allreduce is ~1015 us/call vs star's ~119 us, approximately 8.5x slower. However, this is expected behavior for a 10KB payload on PCIe: the 6 sequential Python-synchronized P2P rounds dominate latency for small payloads. The feature description explicitly acknowledges this, and the validation contract allows documenting this finding rather than requiring ring to be faster.
issues.suggestedFix: The ring allreduce latency target (VAL-RING-004) of < 122 us is unrealistic for PCIe-connected GPUs with Python-level round synchronization. The feature correctly documents and explains this. Consider marking VAL-RING-004 as 'not-applicable' given the explicit CONTEXT note in the ring-allreduce-integration feature description that confirms this is expected.

issues.description: The RingAllreduce._fp16_send_bufs list is allocated but never used in _run_ring_allreduce (the implementation transitioned to FP32 P2P transfers directly). Dead buffer allocation of chunk_bytes_fp16 bytes per GPU.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove self._fp16_send_bufs allocation since the code uses FP32 P2P transfers directly (which is correct for precision). This saves a small amount of VRAM per GPU.

issues.description: The allreduce_residual_async method does NOT achieve true async behavior — _run_ring_allreduce calls stream_synchronize() internally (6x per round × 3 rounds = 18 synchronize calls), which blocks the Python thread. The 'async' in the name is misleading: only the start gate (compute events) is GPU-side async; the ring rounds block the CPU.
issues.suggestedFix: Document in the docstring that allreduce_residual_async for ring topology is functionally synchronous (CPU-blocks during rounds). The benefit over the synchronous allreduce_residual is only the event-gated start. For ring allreduce, async semantics are difficult to achieve with the current multi-round Python synchronization approach.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Ring allreduce on PCIe is significantly slower than star topology for small payloads (hidden_size=5120, 10KB). The 6 sequential P2P rounds with Python-level synchronization dominate latency (~1015 us vs ~119 us for star). Ring allreduce would only be beneficial for hidden_size > ~32768 where bandwidth (not latency) dominates.
sharedStateObservations.suggestedAction: Document in library/architecture.md under ring allreduce section

sharedStateObservations.observation: RingAllreduce uses FP32 scratch buffers for ring reduce-scatter and all-gather phases, with FP16 only in the partial input and final residual output. This achieves max_abs_err=0.0 vs CPU FP32 reference (exact match). The precision is better than star topology which uses FP16 throughout.
sharedStateObservations.suggestedAction: Document precision advantage of FP32 accumulation in ring allreduce

keyMetrics.correctnessMaxAbsErr: 0.0 (exact match vs CPU FP32 reference, all 4 GPUs)
keyMetrics.val_ring_004: NOT MET — ring latency 1015 us >> 122 us target (expected for small payloads)
keyMetrics.val_ring_006: NOT IMPLEMENTED — INT8 partial quantization was not implemented (optional)

summary: Ring allreduce kernel and RingAllreduce class are correctly implemented. Compilation succeeds, correctness is excellent (max_abs_err=0.0 vs CPU FP32 ref), and TP=4 decode cosine similarity (0.999991) passes. The ring topology is slower than star for 10KB payloads as expected — 6 sequential P2P rounds with Python-level sync overhead dominate latency for small payloads on PCIe. VAL-RING-004 (latency < 122 us) is not met but this is expected and documented. Minor: dead _fp16_send_bufs allocation and misleading 'async' naming in allreduce_residual_async. Feature is pass — all critical correctness criteria met.
